course_description:
Where does a news story begin? Sometimes with a rumour that needs checking, often with a question you can't ignore, a detail that feels out of place, or a voice that deserves to be heard.

Journalism starts with curiosity, and it grows through confidence. The confidence to ask difficult questions, verify information under pressure, and communicate clearly when the stakes are high.

JOUR1112 - News Practice is an introduction to that craft. You'll learn how reporters think, how they gather facts, and how they turn uncertainty into accurate, timely stories across text, images, and video. Along the way, you'll practise: analysing sources, spotting weak arguments, speaking to people you’ve never met, and explaining complex issues with precision.

This course is practical. You will pitch ideas, write news stories, analyse evidence, create social-media news content, and produce a final multimedia piece that demonstrates your developing voice. You will begin building the confidence required to operate in fast-paced environments, the ability to investigate a problem, make sense of competing perspectives, and communicate what you've found with clarity.

This semester’s offering of JOUR1112 emphasises learning-by-doing. Across the semester, you will work through a series of structured news writing and reporting exercises that simulate the realities of the newsroom: scanning for leads, making first contact with sources, verifying competing claims, and producing fast, accurate updates as stories develop.

You will work with material drawn from real events in Queensland, practise conducting interviews beyond the classroom, and learn to use evidence, public records, and digital tools to sharpen your reporting. Tutorials will function as small working newsrooms: idea workshops, writing sprints, peer-editing sessions, and fieldwork preparation.

Guest reporters may join selected weeks to discuss news judgment, pitching, and the pressures of working in a live media environment. By the end of the semester, you will have produced a small portfolio of stories across text, photo, video, and social platforms that reflects your growth in confidence and technique, and prepares you for the reporting challenges that follow in later courses.

Additional timetable information
Whilst every effort is made to place students in their preferred activity, it is not always possible for a student to be enrolled in their tutorial of choice. If you require assistance, please ensure that you email

from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: The course aims to provide students with a fundamental understanding of, and practice in, journalistic reporting processes.

learning_outcomes.description: Students will analyse news topics and write a news story.
Students will observe, investigate and gather information for a news story.
Students will learn to pitch a news story in the appropriate format to a news editor.
Students will identify news sources and interview them for publication.
Students will learn to exercise their ethical judgement to report in a fair, balanced and accurate manner.
Students will create a contemporary multimedia news story in the appropriate style and structure.

assessment_details.title: News writing task-Mid-Semester Exam
assessment_details.task_description: You will write two news leads based on information provided in hard copy at the beginning of the assessment. Each lead must capture the central news value of its story with clarity and precision, demonstrating your ability to identify the strongest angle under time pressure. This is a handwritten task. You will compose both leads during the invigilated assessment period using the material supplied on the day. Electronic devices are not permitted unless prior approval has been granted by the course coordinator. Your work must comply with the SCA Style Guide, including spelling, punctuation, syntax and grammar. Failure to meet these style requirements may result in a zero grade for this task. This assessment evaluates your ability to produce accurate, original work without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). The use of such technologies is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 60 minutes Calculator options No calculators permitted Open/closed book Closed book examination - no written materials permitted Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person

Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
If you are unable to complete this exam due to illness or exceptional circumstances, you can apply for a deferred exam in mySI-net. The deferred exam will be held separately on Saturday 16 May 2026. This will be the only opportunity to complete the deferred exam. If you do not complete the deferred exam on Saturday 16 May 2026, you will receive zero marks.

assessment_details.title: News Story Pitch and Story Brief
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Project
assessment_details.task_description: Pitch (your story idea + rationale to editor) - 250 – 350 words - 15% of 30 Submit a pitch for one hyperlocal news story, written in the first person and directed to the editor of your nominated local news outlet. Your pitch should clearly communicate the story idea, why it matters now, and why your audience will care. Treat it as a real professional pitch: concise, purposeful, and grounded in evidence. Your story idea must be original and based on a genuine issue, event, or development within your local community. You must propose at least two primary sources - real people you can contact and interview for Assessment 3. Family members are not permitted. You are encouraged to make initial contact or attempt a short pre-interview where appropriate. You must include a preliminary contact list for your nominated sources (names, roles, and contact details) in your brief. This demonstrates that your reporting plans are feasible and that your sources are accessible. Your pitch should address: Your angle: what the story is really about and why it matters. Newsworthiness: why this is a news story, and what it offers your outlet’s audience. Core facts: the who, what, when, where, why, and how. Your proposed primary sources and why they are the right voices for this story. Relevant secondary material (e.g., public data, statements, reports, online sources). NOT other news stories. You will develop this same idea into a full news story for Assessment 3. Read the Assessment 3 description to ensure you pitch something workable, timely, and reportable. Use of AI: Generative AI is permitted in the development of your written pitch as a writing tool, not as a substitute for editorial judgment. The story itself must originate from your own reporting idea, and all sources must be real people you are able to contact and interview. AI cannot be used to invent sources, quotes, events, or any form of fabricated material. Misuse will be treated as academic misconduct and a fail. Story Brief – 350 – 450 words - 15% of 30 Alongside your pitch, you will submit a detailed story brief compiled as one .pdf file. The purpose of the brief is to demonstrate the depth of your research, the logic behind your angle, and the practical steps you will take to report the story. This document mirrors the briefing notes used by professional journalists and producers when preparing a reporter or presenter for a story. It should stand on its own, clear enough that another journalist could pick it up and understand your rationale, the story context, the sources, and the reporting plan. Your brief should include: Context and significance: What is the wider issue or development your story sits within? Why does it matter now? Background research: Key facts, recent updates, relevant data, policy, or history that shape the story. Stakeholder map: The individuals, groups, or institutions affected by or involved in the story. Primary sources: Who they are Why they matter What perspectives or information you expect from them Evidence that you can access them (contact details, emails, phone calls, visit attempts, etc.) Interview strategy: Key questions or themes you intend to explore Anticipated lines of inquiry Any ethical considerations or sensitivities Verification plan: How you intend to check claims, confirm facts, and address conflicting accounts. Reporting plan: When and how you intend to complete interviews, gather multimedia elements, and produce your final story. Potential risks/constraints: Access issues, legal considerations, safety concerns, or reporting obstacles you may need to navigate. Proposed story structure (early draft): How you currently imagine your story unfolding, your likely lead, key moments, and narrative shape. The brief must be written in professional, third-person voice. It is not part of the pitch and will be assessed as a separate component of Assessment 2. Use of AI: Students may use AI as part of their writing and planning process, but the brief must reflect your own research, reporting initiative, and editorial judgment. AI must not be used to create fictitious facts, sources, quotes, or events. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Final News Story: Story, Images and Video
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 3 — Final News Story, Images and Video (500–650 words), 50% Produce a hyperlocal news story about an event or issue in the community where you live. Your story may be written in news or feature style, but it must follow professional journalistic conventions and demonstrate depth, accuracy, and strong news judgment. The story should be 500–650 words and must be grounded in original reporting. Your written story must include a minimum of three primary sources whom you have interviewed for this assessment. You may draw on any number of secondary sources - public data, media statements, documents, online material, provided they are used responsibly and clearly attributed within the story. Academic referencing is not permitted; all attribution must follow journalistic style. Your accompanying two images should strengthen the story visually and be suitable for publication alongside the piece in the news outlet you identified in Assessment 2. Each photograph must include a caption with accurate identification of people and relevant context. Captions are not included in the word count. You will also produce a 30–45 second video summary or a supporting angle of your story designed for social media distribution. This should be in a format appropriate for TikTok or Instagram Reels and should match the tone and style of your nominated outlet. The video does not need to feature the sources interviewed for the written story (but if appropriate and you've secured their consent, then it may include those sources); it may be a piece-to-camera or an edited visual explainer. It must, however, communicate the core of your story clearly and professionally. Upload the video file to Turnitin or as instructed on Blackboard. Parody or satire is not permitted. Submit an appendix containing: A contact list for all primary sources interviewed, including details of how contact was made. A reference list of secondary sources (hyperlinks only), providing clear, accessible documentation of all supporting material. Academic referencing formats are not required. Use of AI: Generative AI may be used for limited purposes such as proofreading, clarifying expression, or testing structural ideas. It must not be used to fabricate events, sources, quotes, or reporting. The story must reflect your own journalistic decision-making, voice, and reporting practice. Misuse of AI including invented evidence or synthetic interviews will be treated as academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via Blackboard. Students are permitted to submit their story in another media platform (eg. sound, vision) if they obtain prior approval from their tutor or course coordinator.

The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1 Lecture: Introductions Introduction to Reporting Lecture: What journalists do How stories begin Course overview, assessments, and expectations The reporting mindset: curiosity, accuracy, and confidence Learning outcomes: L01
Week 1: NO TUTORIALS Tutorials start in Week 2
Week 2 Lecture: News Values & Finding Stories What makes something news How reporters identify leads, angles, gaps Hyperlocal journalism Learning outcomes: L02
Week 2 Tutorial: How to find a story in your local area News Analysis and News values workshop Intro to writing simple leads Learning outcomes: L01
Week 3 Lecture: Writing the News: Leads, Angles, and Structure The inverted pyramid Writing leads under pressure Turning facts into a coherent story Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 3 Tutorial: Lead-writing drills News Analysis and News values workshop Identifying the best angle Learning outcomes: L01

Week 4 Lecture: Verification, Fact-Checking & Information Safety How journalists verify their stories Tools and techniques for checking information Identifying misinformation Basics of digital safety for reporters Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Week 4 Tutorial: Final lead writing drills News Analysis and News values workshop Verification exercises using real examples Learning outcomes: L01
Week 5 Lecture: Interviewing for News Preparing for interviews Asking the right questions Handling difficult exchanges Recording accurate information Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Week 5 Tutorial: Practice interviews Peer role-play Designing questions for Assessment 2 (pitch + brief) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Week 6 Lecture: Social Media Reporting & Live Updating • Reporting on TikTok, Instagram • Live updates and accuracy under pressure • Building trust and credibility online Learning outcomes: L06
Week 6 Tutorial: NO TUTORIALS due to public holiday NO TUTORIALS due to public holiday
Mid Semester Break
Week 7 Lecture: Inside the newsroom Inside the newsroom How stories are commissioned Working under deadlines Learning outcomes: L01
Week 7 Tutorial: Preparing for Assessment 2 Breaking down what makes a strong pitch Drafting and refining angles Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Week 8 Lecture: Visual Storytelling Principles of visual journalism Storytelling through images and sequences What makes a great social video Learning outcomes: L05, L06

Week 8 Tutorial: • Creating visual narratives • Using phones to capture reporting-ready visuals • Writing updates for social platforms • Captions, headlines, micro-format writing • Turning a lead into a short update • Planning the video component of Assessment 3 Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Week 9 Lecture: Multi-Platform Journalism & Converged Reporting Text, images, audio, video: shaping one story for different platforms The role of the modern reporter Learning outcomes: L06
Week 9 Tutorial: Drafting story structures Refining interview plans for Assessment 3 Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 10 Lecture: Crafting the Final Story: Bringing It All Together Final tips: structure, clarity, verification, attribution, ethics Common mistakes and how to avoid them Ensuring your reporting is publishable Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05
Week 10 Tutorial: Workshop Workshop for Story Writing and Structure Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05, L06
Week 11 Lecture: Inside the newsroom Inside the newsroom How social media stories are produced Working under deadlines Learning outcomes: L05, L06
Week 11 Tutorial: Workshop: story + images + video planning Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05, L06
Week 12 Lecture: Open Workshop for Assessment related queries Open Workshop for Assessment related queries Learning outcomes: L02, L04
Week 12 Tutorial: A final opportunity to receive feedback on your final assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05, L06
Week 13: NO LECTURE OR TUTORIALS

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses immediately or outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). If your email is urgent, include "URGENT" in your subject heading Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
